assessment_items.question_content:
**Fuseli's*The Nightmare* was influential on what literary genre?**   


![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/ae6a0952687565ad5b61349cdf976041.jpg)

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
***The Nightmare* became a defining image of Gothic horror, inspiring such writers as Mary Shelley and Edgar Allan Poe among others.  **

**[Read this text to review.](/humanities/art-history/art-history-1800-1848-industrial-revolution-i/romanticism-in-england/a/henry-fuselis-the-nightmare-1781)**

assessment_items.question_content:
**Created in 1781, how did Fuseli's painting differ from most paintings exhibited at London's royal academy during the Enlightenment?  **

![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/ae6a0952687565ad5b61349cdf976041.jpg)

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: **Human anatomy was often idealized by academic artists, trained in the established tradition of classical art.  **

Fuseli's scene was entirely invented and lacked the moralizing subject matter typical of Neoclassical art of the Enlightenment period.   

**[Read this text to review.](/humanities/art-history/art-history-1800-1848-industrial-revolution-i/romanticism-in-england/a/henry-fuselis-the-nightmare-1781)**

choices.content: It revealed the artist's classical knowledge.
It relied on high contrasts of light and dark.
It idealized the anatomy of the female figure.
It was based on a literary source.

assessment_items.question_content:
**The original definition of a "nightmare" is best embodied by the figure of  [[☃ dropdown 1]] in Fuseli's composition.**

![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/ae6a0952687565ad5b61349cdf976041.jpg)
hints:
The title, "The Nightmare" refers to now obsolete definition of the term.  The figure of the incubus embodies this concept of an evil spirit that tortures humans while they sleep, and was perceived as a pressure on the chest while asleep. 

**[Read this text to review.](/humanities/art-history/art-history-1800-1848-industrial-revolution-i/romanticism-in-england/a/henry-fuselis-the-nightmare-1781)**

assessment_items.question_content:
**The widespread distribution of Fuseli's *The Nightmare* resulted from $\_\_\_\_\_\_$**

![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/ae6a0952687565ad5b61349cdf976041.jpg)

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
*The Nightmare* was engraved by Thomas Burke two years after its production and the year following its exhibition at the Royal Academy.  Its availability as a print at a relatively low price resulted in its distribution to a wider audience. 

**[Read this text to review.](/humanities/art-history/art-history-1800-1848-industrial-revolution-i/romanticism-in-england/a/henry-fuselis-the-nightmare-1781)**

choices.content: its production as a less expensive print.
its exhibition at the Royal Academy.
later versions of the painting by the artist.
its use as a satirical image.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Compare the use of light in these paintings.   Indicate whether it serves the functions below in one, both, or neither of these examples.  **

Henry Fuseli,* The Nightmare*, 1781

![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/59ee31f991aaf8439a5bf4e46cb4cdc4.jpg)

Joseph Wright of Derby, *A Philosopher Giving a Lecture at the Orrery (in which a lamp is put in place of the sun)*, c. 1763-65,

![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/716d0e0f1e48c3303342103f4bc6c411.jpg)

[[☃ categorizer 1]]
hints: Both Wright and Fuseli rely on the formal element of light to contribute drama and focus the viewer's attention.

Unlike the slightly earlier painting by Joseph Wright of Derby, where light symbolizes the enlightening power of observation and inquiry to yield knowledge, Fuseli’s *The Nightmare* instead shows the futility of light to penetrate or explain the darker realms of the unconscious.  Light does not symbolize the presence of God in either painting.

**[Read this text to review.](/humanities/art-history/art-history-1800-1848-industrial-revolution-i/romanticism-in-england/a/henry-fuselis-the-nightmare-1781)**

items: Light symbolizes the presence of God.
LIght calls attention to details in the work.
Light implies knowledge gained through inquiry.
Light suggests some things cannot be understood.
Light adds drama to the scene.
